home.title: Seeing Single-Cell
home.subtitle: An interactive visual exploration of the mathematics behind
home.subtitleEnd: Understand the math through biology. See the logic behind every line of code.
home.interactDesc: Adjust sliders, hover over visualizations, and watch parameters change in real-time.
home.readDesc: Each section explains a concept, then shows it visually. Watch the math come alive.
home.experimentDesc: Change parameters and see the effects immediately. Understand WHY, not just WHAT.
home.ch4Desc: From thousands of dimensions to a 2D map. Understand PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP through interactive visualizations.
home.ch5Desc: From neighborhood graphs to cell communities. Understand KNN construction, Louvain community detection, and cluster evaluation through interactive animations.
home.ch6Desc: From high-dimensional space to a 2D map. Understand t-SNE and UMAP through Swiss Roll animations.

ch1.title: The Gene Expression Matrix
ch1.subtitle: Every single-cell experiment begins with a matrix — then we explore how values are distributed across cells and genes.
ch1.step1Title: What Does the Data Look Like?
ch1.step2Title: Why Are There So Many Zeros?
ch1.bioZeroDesc: The gene is truly not expressed in that cell.
ch1.techDropoutDesc: The gene was expressed, but at such low levels that sequencing failed to capture it.
ch1.conceptText1: In scRNA-seq, we measure the expression level of each gene in every cell.
ch1.conceptText5: The result is a matrix where each cell (row) has a unique pattern of gene expression.
ch1.sparseDesc1: Look at the matrix above — notice the many white (zero) cells? This is called
ch1.sparseDesc2: and it’s a hallmark of single-cell data.
ch1.sparseDesc4: of all values are zero. This happens for two main reasons:
ch1.bioZeroFullDesc: The gene is truly not expressed in that cell. Cell-type specific genes will show zeros in cells where they are inactive.
ch1.techDropoutFullDesc: The gene was expressed, but at such low levels that the sequencing process failed to capture it. This is a key challenge in scRNA-seq analysis.
ch1.mathDesc4: is the number of cells and
ch1.mathDesc5: is the number of genes. The sparsity is defined as:
ch1.readRowsDesc: is a single cell. Cells of the same type (e.g., CD4 T cells) tend to have similar expression patterns — they’ll cluster together visually.
ch1.readColsDesc1: is a gene. Marker genes like
ch1.readColsDesc2: are highly expressed only in specific cell types, creating bright vertical “stripes” in the matrix.
ch1.tryItDesc: Hover over the matrix to see individual values. Click on a row or column to highlight it. Use the color scale slider to adjust contrast.
ch1.look1: Rows with similar patterns → same cell type
ch1.look2: Columns with many zeros → lowly expressed or cell-type specific
ch1.look3: Bright “hotspots” → marker genes in specific cell types
ch1.libSizeDefDesc: The total sum of UMI (Unique Molecular Identifier) counts across all genes for each cell. UMIs are short barcodes attached to each mRNA molecule before sequencing, allowing us to count individual molecules rather than PCR duplicates. This is the baseline measurement of sequencing depth.
ch1.exprFeaturesDefDesc: The number of genes with non-zero counts in each cell. Distinct from the total count — a cell may have high total counts but few unique genes.
ch1.sparsityExplain: Where Xᵢⱼ is the expression of gene j in cell i, n is number of cells, p is number of genes, and 𝟙[·] is the indicator function (1 if condition is true, 0 otherwise).

ch2.title: Quality Control & Filtering
ch2.subtitle: Not all genes are created equal. Explore how expression values are distributed across cells.
ch2.concept1: The distribution of a gene's expression values across all cells reveals important patterns. Housekeeping genes tend to be normally distributed, while cell-type markers show bimodal distributions (on or off).
ch2.try1: Select different genes from the dropdown — compare CD3D (T cell marker) vs B2M (housekeeping)
ch2.try2: Adjust the bin count to see how granularity affects the histogram's shape
ch2.try3: Toggle the KDE curve on/off to see how kernel smoothing works
ch2.try4: Watch how the statistics panel changes with each gene
ch2.histDesc: A histogram divides the data range into
ch2.histDescEnd: and counts how many values fall into each bin. It's simple and intuitive, but the shape depends on bin width and starting position.
ch2.kdeDesc: KDE places a smooth kernel (usually Gaussian) at each data point, then sums them up. The result is a continuous, smooth estimate of the probability density function.
ch2.bandwidthDesc2: in KDE controls the smoothness: too small → noisy and spiky, too large → over-smoothed. In the visualization above, try different genes to see how the optimal bandwidth changes with the data shape.
ch2.normalPatDesc: Housekeeping genes (e.g., GAPDH, B2M) are expressed in all cells. Their distribution is roughly symmetric around the mean.
ch2.bimodalPatDesc: Cell-type markers (e.g., CD3D) show two peaks: one at zero (cells that don't express it) and one at a higher value (cells that do).
ch2.zeroInfPatDesc: Lowly expressed genes have a huge spike at zero with a long right tail. This is the "dropout" problem in scRNA-seq.
ch2.kdeFormulaDesc: where f̂(x) is the estimated density, n is the number of cells, h is the bandwidth (smoothing parameter), K is the Gaussian kernel function, and x₁...xₙ are the observed expression values.
ch2.madDesc: MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) measures the median distance of each data point from the median. It is robust to outliers, unlike standard deviation. Points beyond 3 MADs from the median are considered outliers.
ch2.scatterMtDesc: The nCount vs percent_mt scatter plot reveals damaged cells: cells with low total UMIs but high mitochondrial percentage are likely broken (mRNA leaked out). Cells in the bottom-left are healthy.

ch3.title: Preprocessing Trilogy
ch3.subtitle: Before clustering and visualization, scRNA-seq data goes through three essential preprocessing steps:
ch3.subtitleEnd: These steps correct technical noise and prepare the data for downstream analysis.
ch3.whyNormDesc: Different cells capture different amounts of mRNA during sequencing — this is a
ch3.whyNormDesc2: not biological. Cell A might have 10× more total counts than Cell B simply because it was more efficiently captured.
ch3.formulaDesc: 1) Divide by library size, × 10k  2) log1p to stabilize variance
ch3.interactDesc: Hover over the matrix to see cell details
ch3.tryNorm1: Toggle Library Size — cells become comparable
ch3.tryNorm2: Toggle × 10,000 — values scale to interpretable range
ch3.tryNorm3: Try log1p / log2 / ln — see how log compresses dynamic range
ch3.whyHVGDesc: Not all genes are informative. Housekeeping genes (like GAPDH) are expressed at similar levels across all cells. HVGs show high cell-to-cell variance and drive biological heterogeneity.
ch3.hvgInput: Data input: normalized expression matrix. HVGs identified by mean-variance relationship.
ch3.hvgHeatmapTitle: HVG Expression Across Cells (sorted by type)
ch3.hvgHeatmapDesc: Cells sorted by type. HVGs show heterogeneous expression across cells; uniform genes look the same everywhere.
ch3.poissonDesc: Points above dashed orange line have overdispersion
ch3.nbDesc: Points above purple line are most variable
ch3.whyScaleDesc: After normalization, genes still have different means. Scaling z-scores each gene to mean=0, std=1, giving equal weight in PCA.
ch3.scaleInput: Input: 20 HVGs from log-normalized data. We use a fixed set of top HVGs by variance for this demo.
ch3.subtitleFull: Before clustering and visualization, scRNA-seq data goes through three essential preprocessing steps:
ch3.step1Desc: Different cells capture different amounts of mRNA — this is a technical artifact, not biological. Cell A might have 10× more total counts than Cell B simply because it was more efficiently captured.
ch3.step2Desc: Not all genes are informative. Housekeeping genes (like GAPDH) are expressed at similar levels across all cells. HVGs show high cell-to-cell variance and drive biological heterogeneity.
ch3.hvgDataInput: Data input: normalized expression matrix. HVGs identified by mean-variance relationship.
ch3.poissonDesc2: Points above dashed orange line have overdispersion
ch3.nbDesc2: Points above purple line are most variable
ch3.step3Desc: After normalization, genes still have different means. Scaling z-scores each gene to mean=0, std=1, giving equal weight in PCA.
ch3.scaleFormulaDesc: Input: 20 HVGs from normalized matrix. Output: centered and scaled.
ch3.hvgExprTitle: HVG Expression Across Cells (sorted by type)
ch3.whyLogDesc1: Differences in log-values represent log-fold changes in expression. This is crucial for downstream analyses (PCA, clustering) based on Euclidean distances.
ch3.whyLogConclusion: Log-transformation promotes contributions from genes with strong relative differences, making biologically meaningful changes stand out.
ch3.compBiasDesc: When gene X is upregulated in cell A, sequencing resources are diverted to X, reducing coverage of all other non-DE genes. The net effect: non-DE genes in A appear downregulated compared to B.
ch3.compBiasWhy: This is why library size normalization alone is insufficient — it cannot distinguish between true downregulation and composition bias.
ch3.logFCDesc: log₂(x₂/x₁) = log₂(x₂) - log₂(x₁). The log-transformation converts multiplicative relationships into additive ones, which is what distance-based methods expect.
ch3.sizeFactorDesc: Each cell's size factor = its library size ÷ mean library size. After normalization, all cells have the same effective library size, making them directly comparable.
ch3.varDecompFormula: Total Variance = Technical Component + Biological Component
ch3.varDecompDesc: The trend line in the mean-variance plot estimates the technical component (sampling noise + technical variation). The biological component = total variance − technical component. Genes with the largest biological component are the most informative HVGs.
ch3.negBio: Negative biological components are normal — approximately half of genes will lie below the trend line.
ch3.equalWeightDesc: Without scaling, highly-expressed genes (like ribosomal genes) dominate PCA and clustering simply because of their magnitude, not because they carry the most biological information. Scaling ensures each gene contributes proportionally to its variance, not its expression level.
ch3.regressOutDesc: ScaleData can also regress out unwanted sources of variation, such as mitochondrial contamination (percent.mt) or cell cycle effects, further cleaning the data for downstream analysis.

ch4.title: Principal Component Analysis
ch4.subtitle: From 52 dimensions to a 2D map. Understand how PCA finds the most informative directions in high-dimensional space — and why this matters for identifying cell types.
ch4.subtitleFull: After preprocessing, we have a clean matrix. But how do we see structure in 52 dimensions? PCA finds the directions of greatest variance, projecting data onto a space we can visualize and interpret.
ch4.step1Why: Why can’t we just plot genes directly?
ch4.step1WhyDesc: We have 100 cells × 20 genes. But a human can only see 2-3 dimensions. Trying to visualize 20 dimensions at once is like trying to read 20 books simultaneously. Dimensionality reduction compresses this into a 2D map while preserving the most important structure.
ch4.step1AnalogyDesc: Imagine describing a city using latitude, longitude, altitude, temperature, humidity, population density, noise level... A map combines the most important information into 2 dimensions. PCA does the same for gene expression data.
ch4.step1TryDesc: Below, each scatter plot shows cells using two genes as axes. Some gene pairs separate cell types well (like CD3D vs CD19 — T cells vs B cells). Others don’t. But with 20 genes, there are 190 possible pairs. Which one is the best view? PCA finds it automatically.
ch4.step2WhyVarianceDesc: A gene that shows the same value in every cell tells us nothing. A gene that varies across cells might reveal different cell types. Variance = information content. PCA finds the directions that maximize this variance.
ch4.step2DirectionDesc: If we must project our data onto ONE line, which direction captures the most spread? The answer: the direction along which the data varies the most. That’s exactly what the first principal component (PC1) is.
ch4.step2TryDesc: Drag the slider to rotate the projection line. Watch how the projected points spread out differently. The direction that maximizes spread is PC1!
ch4.step3FormulaDesc: PCA finds a new coordinate system. Each principal component is a weighted combination of the original genes.
ch4.step3Step1Desc: Subtract the mean of each gene. This centers the cloud of points at the origin.
ch4.step3Step2Desc: Compute how genes co-vary. The covariance matrix captures the shape of the data cloud.
ch4.step3Step3Desc: Find eigenvectors (directions) and eigenvalues (importance) of the covariance matrix. The eigenvectors define the new axes; eigenvalues tell us how much variance each captures.
ch4.step3Step4Desc: Multiply the centered data by the eigenvectors. This rotates the data to the new coordinate system.
ch4.step3TryDesc: Toggle each step to see how the data transforms. Watch the data cloud rotate into alignment with the principal components.
ch4.step4VarianceDesc: Each PC captures a fraction of the total variance. PC1 captures the most, PC2 the second most, and so on. The bar chart below shows how much information each PC holds.
ch4.step4ScatterDesc: View cells in PC space. Each point is a cell, colored by its type. Select different PC combinations to see which pairs best separate cell types.
ch4.step4LoadingsDesc: Which genes contribute most to each PC? The loadings table shows the weight of each gene in each principal component. High absolute values mean strong contribution.
ch4.step4Try1: Switch between PC1 vs PC2 and PC2 vs PC3 — notice which separates cell types better
ch4.step4Try2: Check the gene loadings to understand which genes drive each PC
ch4.step4Try3: The variance explained tells you how many PCs you need to retain most information

ch5.title: KNN Graph Clustering
ch5.subtitle: From PC space to cell types. Learn how KNN graphs and Louvain community discovery automatically identify cell populations.
ch5.subtitleFull: We have cells in PC space. Now: can we automatically find groups of similar cells? KNN graph clustering builds a neighbor network, then discovers communities within it.
ch5.step1WhyDesc: You have the gene expression profiles of two cells. How do you decide if they are the same type? The answer: measure the distance between their expression vectors.
ch5.step1EuclideanDesc: Like measuring the absolute difference in taste. If Cell A has CD3D=5 and Cell B has CD3D=2, the difference is 3. Sum this across all genes.
ch5.step1CosineDesc: Like comparing the pattern. Two cells might have very different total expression, but if the ratio of genes is similar, they are the same type.
ch5.step1TryDesc: Select two cells below and compare their gene expression profiles. Watch the distance metrics update in real time.
ch5.step2WhatDesc: For each cell, find its K nearest neighbors. Connect them with edges. This creates a graph that captures the local structure of the data.
ch5.step2TryDesc: Drag the K slider and watch the network change. Too small K — fragmented. Too large K — everything connected.
ch5.step3WhatDesc: The Louvain algorithm finds communities by maximizing modularity: it keeps merging groups if the result has more internal connections than expected by chance.
ch5.step3ModularityDesc: Q measures how much more connected the communities are than random. Q > 0.3 means clear community structure.
ch5.step3TryDesc: Press Play to watch the algorithm iterate. Each round, cells with the same color belong to the same community.
ch5.step4ConfusionDesc: Rows = true cell types, Columns = algorithm clusters. Bright squares mean good agreement.
ch5.step4Try1: Drag the resolution slider — watch clusters merge or split
ch5.step4Try2: Set K very small (2) — see fragmentation
ch5.step4Try3: Compare the confusion matrix at different resolutions

ch6.title: t-SNE & UMAP
ch6.subtitle: From linear to non-linear. Learn how t-SNE and UMAP unfold curved data into readable 2D maps.
ch6.subtitleFull: PCA finds straight-line projections. But biology isn't linear. t-SNE and UMAP are non-linear methods that can unfold curved structures.
ch6.step1WhyDesc: PCA finds the best straight-line projection. But if the data has curved structure, linear methods crush layers together.
ch6.step1TryDesc: Below, the same dataset is projected by three methods. PCA fails to separate, t-SNE and UMAP succeed.
ch6.step2CoreDesc: t-SNE converts distances to probabilities, then minimizes the difference between high-dim and low-dim probabilities.
ch6.step2TryDesc: Press Play to watch points move from random to clusters. Adjust Perplexity to see its effect.
ch6.step3CoreDesc: UMAP builds a topological graph in high dimensions, then reconstructs it in 2D. Unlike t-SNE, it preserves some global distance relationships.
ch6.step3TryDesc: Adjust n_neighbors (local vs global) and min_dist (cluster tightness).
ch6.step4CompareDesc: Run t-SNE multiple times to see it gives different results. UMAP and PCA are more stable.
ch6.step4Pitfall1: Inter-cluster distances are meaningless in t-SNE/UMAP
ch6.step4Pitfall2: t-SNE results change with different random seeds
ch6.step4Pitfall3: Never cluster on t-SNE coords — cluster in PC space first
ch6.step4Pitfall4: Point density doesn't represent true cell density

home.title: 看见单细胞
home.subtitle: 用可视化交互理解
home.subtitleRest: 背后的数学原理。在生物场景中理解数据、参数、分布和算法。
home.ch1Desc: 每个单细胞实验都从一个矩阵开始——行是细胞，列是基因，每个值告诉我们基因的表达量。
home.ch2Desc: 识别并去除低质量细胞——空液滴、破损细胞和双胞体。
home.ch3Desc: 对数标准化、筛选高变基因、z分数中心化——校正技术噪声，为下游分析做准备。
home.ch4Desc: 从数千维度到2D地图。通过交互式可视化理解PCA。
home.how1Desc: 调整滑块、悬停可视化图表，实时观察参数变化对结果的影响。
home.how2Desc: 每个板块先解释概念，再用可视化呈现。让代码背后的逻辑变得直观可见。
home.how3Desc: 改变参数，立即看到效果。理解为什么这样做，而不仅仅是怎么做。
home.subtitleEnd: 在生物场景中理解数据、参数、分布和算法。
home.interactDesc: 调整滑块、悬停可视化图表，实时观察参数变化对结果的影响。
home.readDesc: 每个板块先解释概念，再用可视化呈现。让代码背后的逻辑变得直观可见。
home.experimentDesc: 改变参数，立即看到效果。理解为什么这样做，而不仅仅是怎么做。
home.ch5Desc: 从邻域图到细胞社群。通过交互式动画理解KNN构建、Louvain社区发现和聚类评估。
home.ch6Desc: 从高维空间到2D地图。通过瑞士卷动画理解t-SNE和UMAP的原理与差异。

ch1.title: 基因表达矩阵
ch1.subtitleFull: 每个单细胞实验都从一个矩阵开始。让我们理解这个矩阵的结构、含义和数学特性。
ch1.matrixDesc: 这是一个100×52的矩阵。行是细胞，列是基因，每个值是该基因在该细胞中的UMI计数。
ch1.sparsityDesc: 大部分值是0。这是因为每个细胞只表达了所有基因的一小部分。
ch1.subtitle: 每个单细胞实验都从一个矩阵开始——然后探索表达值在细胞和基因间的分布模式。
ch1.bioZeroFullDesc: 该基因在该细胞中确实不表达。细胞类型特异性基因在不活跃的细胞中显示为零。
ch1.conceptText1: 在单细胞测序中，我们测量每个基因在每个细胞中的表达量。
ch1.conceptText5: 结果是一个矩阵，每个细胞（行）都有独特的基因表达模式。
ch1.exprFeaturesDefDesc: 每个细胞中非零表达的基因数量。与总计数不同——一个细胞可能有很高的总表达量但只有很少的独特基因。
ch1.libSizeDefDesc: 每个细胞所有基因的UMI（唯一分子标识符）计数总和。UMI是测序前附加在每个mRNA分子上的短序列标签，让我们能区分真实的分子数量与PCR重复。这是测序深度的基础测量。
ch1.sparseDesc1: 看看上面的矩阵——注意到很多白色（零值）单元格了吗？这叫做
ch1.sparseDesc2: ，这是单细胞数据的标志特征。
ch1.sparseDesc4: 的所有值为零。这主要有两个原因：
ch1.sparsityExplain: 其中 Xᵢⱬ 是基因 j 在细胞 i 中的表达量，n 是细胞数，p 是基因数，ƚ[·] 是指示函数（条件为真时取1，否则取0）。
ch1.techDropoutFullDesc: 基因确实表达了，但表达量太低，测序过程未能捕获它。这是单细胞测序分析中的关键挑战。

ch2.title: 质控与过滤
ch2.subtitle: 并非所有基因都是平等的。探索表达值在细胞间的分布模式。
ch2.step2Title: 直方图 vs 核密度估计
ch2.concept1: 基因在所有细胞中的表达值分布揭示了重要模式。管家基因倾向于呈正态分布，而细胞类型标记基因显示双峰分布（开或关）。
ch2.try1: 从下拉菜单选择不同的基因——比较CD3D（T细胞标记）和B2M（管家基因）
ch2.try2: 调整分箱数量，观察粒度如何影响直方图形状
ch2.try3: 开关KDE曲线，观察核平滑的效果
ch2.try4: 观察统计面板如何随每个基因变化
ch2.histDesc: 直方图将数据范围分成
ch2.histDescEnd: ，并计算每个箱子中有多少值。它简单直观，但形状取决于箱宽和起始位置。
ch2.kdeDesc: KDE在每个数据点放置一个平滑核（通常为高斯核），然后将它们求和。结果是概率密度函数的连续平滑估计。
ch2.bandwidthDesc2: 控制KDE的平滑度：太小→噪声大且尖刺多，太大→过度平滑。在上面的可视化中，尝试不同基因观察最优带宽如何随数据形状变化。
ch2.normalPatDesc: 管家基因（如GAPDH、B2M）在所有细胞中表达。它们的分布大致围绕均值对称。
ch2.bimodalPatDesc: 细胞类型标记基因（如CD3D）显示两个峰值：一个在零处（不表达的细胞），一个在较高值处（表达的细胞）。
ch2.zeroInfPatDesc: 低表达基因在零处有巨大尖峰，右侧有长尾。这是scRNA-seq中的“丢失”问题。
ch2.kdeFormulaDesc: 其中 f̂(x) 是估计密度，n 是细胞数，h 是带宽（平滑参数），K 是高斯核函数，x₁...xₙ 是观测到的表达值。
ch2.madDesc: MAD（中位数绝对偏差）衡量每个数据点距离中位数的中位距离。与标准差不同，MAD对异常值更加鲁棒。距离中位数超过3倍MAD的点被认为是异常值。
ch2.scatterMtDesc: nCount vs percent_mt 散点图揭示破损细胞：总UMI数低但线粒体比例高的细胞可能已经破损（mRNA泄漏）。左下角的细胞是健康的。

ch3.title: 预处理三部曲
ch3.subtitleFull: 在聚类和可视化之前，单细胞RNA测序数据需要经过三个关键预处理步骤：
ch3.subtitle: 对数标准化、筛选高变基因、z分数中心化——校正技术噪声，为下游分析做准备。
ch3.compBiasDesc: 当基因X在细胞A中上调时，测序资源被转移到X，减少了所有其他非差异表达基因的覆盖度。净效应：A中的非DE基因看起来比B中下调了。
ch3.compBiasWhy: 这就是为什么仅靠文库大小标准化是不够的——它无法区分真正的下调和组成偏差。
ch3.equalWeightDesc: 如果不进行scaling，高表达基因（如核糖体基因）仅凭其量级就主导了PCA和聚类，而不是因为它们携带最多的生物学信息。Scaling确保每个基因按其方差而非表达水平成比例地贡献。
ch3.formulaDesc: 1) 除以文库大小，×10k  2) log1p稳定方差
ch3.hvgDataInput: 数据输入：归一化后的表达矩阵。HVG通过均值-方差关系识别。
ch3.logFCDesc: log₂(x₂/x₁) = log₂(x₂) - log₂(x₁)。对数变换将乘法关系转换为加法关系，这正是基于距离的方法所需要的。
ch3.nbDesc2: 紫色线上方的点变异性最大
ch3.negBio: 负的生物学组分是正常的——大约一半的基因会位于趋势线以下。
ch3.poissonDesc2: 橙色虚线上方的点存在过度离散
ch3.regressOut: 回归去除不需要的变异
ch3.regressOutDesc: ScaleData还可以回归去除不需要的变异来源，如线粒体污染（percent.mt）或细胞周期效应，进一步清理数据供下游分析。
ch3.scaleFormulaDesc: 输入：来自标准化矩阵的20个HVG。输出：居中并缩放。
ch3.sizeFactorDesc: 每个细胞的size factor = 其文库大小 ÷ 平均文库大小。标准化后，所有细胞具有相同的等效文库大小，使它们可以直接比较。
ch3.step1Desc: 不同细胞在测序过程中捕获不同量的mRNA——这是技术假象，而非生物学差异。细胞A的总表达量可能是细胞B的10倍，仅仅因为它被更高效地捕获了。
ch3.step2Desc: 并非所有基因都具有信息量。管家基因（如GAPDH）在所有细胞中表达水平相似。HVG显示高细胞间方差，驱动生物学异质性。
ch3.step3Desc: 中心化后，基因仍有不同的均值。Scaling对每个基因进行z-score中心化，使均值=0，标准差=1，在PCA中给予相等权重。
ch3.sumNormDesc: 文库大小校正 + 对数变换
ch3.sumScaleDesc: 均值=0, 标准差=1, 为PCA准备
ch3.tryNorm1: 切换文库大小——细胞变得可比较
ch3.tryNorm2: 切换× 10,000——值缩放到可解释范围
ch3.tryNorm3: 尝试log1p / log2 / ln——观察对数如何压缩动态范围
ch3.varDecompDesc: 均值-方差图中的趋势线估计了技术组分（采样噪声 + 技术变异）。生物学组分 = 总方差 - 技术组分。具有最大生物学组分的基因是最具信息量的HVG。
ch3.whyLogConclusion: 对数变换促进了具有强相对差异的基因的贡献，使生物学上有意义的变化脱颖而出。
ch3.whyLogDesc1: 对数值的差异代表表达的对数倍变化。这对于基于欧氏距离的下游分析（PCA、聚类）至关重要。

ch4.title: 主成分分析
ch4.subtitleFull: 预处理之后，我们有了干净的矩阵。但如何在52维中看到结构？PCA找到方差最大的方向。
ch4.step1Why: 为什么不能直接用基因画图？
ch4.step1WhyDesc: 我们有100个细胞 × 20个基因。但人类只能看到2-3个维度。
ch4.step1TryDesc: 下面的散点图用两个基因作为坐标轴展示细胞。
ch4.subtitle: 从52维到2D地图。理解PCA如何找到高维空间中最具信息量的方向。
ch4.step1AnalogyDesc: 想象用纬度、经度、海拔、温度、湿度、人口密度、噪音水平来描述一个城市……地图将最重要的信息压缩到2维。PCA对基因表达数据做同样的事。
ch4.step2DirectionDesc: 如果必须将数据投影到一条线上，哪个方向能捕获最多散布？答案：数据变化最大的方向。这就是第一主成分（PC1）。
ch4.step2TryDesc: 拖动滑块旋转投影线。观察投影点如何不同地展开。最大化散布的方向就是PC1！
ch4.step2WhyVarianceDesc: 在每个细胞中显示相同值的基因什么也没有告诉我们。在细胞间变化的基因可能揭示不同的细胞类型。方差 = 信息量。PCA找到最大化这个方差的方向。
ch4.step3FormulaDesc: PCA找到一个新的坐标系。每个主成分是原始基因的加权组合。
ch4.step3Step1Desc: 减去每个基因的均值。这将数据点云中心移到原点。
ch4.step3Step2Desc: 计算基因如何协同变化。协方差矩阵捕获数据云的形状。
ch4.step3Step3Desc: 找到协方差矩阵的特征向量（方向）和特征值（重要性）。特征向量定义新轴；特征值告诉我们每个轴捕获了多少方差。
ch4.step3Step4Desc: 将居中数据乘以特征向量。这将数据旋转到新坐标系。
ch4.step3TryDesc: 切换每个步骤观察数据如何变换。观察数据云旋转对齐主成分。
ch4.step4Try2: 悬停查看细胞类型和载荷

ch5.title: KNN图聚类
ch5.subtitleFull: PC空间到细胞类型。学习KNN图和Louvain社区发现如何自动识别细胞群体。
ch5.step1WhyDesc: 你拿到两个细胞的基因表达谱。怎么判断它们是不是同一类？答案：测量距离。
ch5.step1TryDesc: 在下方选择两个细胞，比较它们的基因表达谱。
ch5.subtitle: 从距离到社区。通过KNN图构建、Louvain社区发现和聚类评估，理解无监督细胞分群的完整流程。
ch5.step1CosineDesc: 测量两个向量之间的角度。值范围0-2，0表示完全相同方向。对表达量的绝对大小不敏感。
ch5.step1EuclideanDesc: 两点之间的直线距离。受表达量绝对大小影响——高表达基因的变化会导致更大的距离。
ch5.step2TryDesc: 调整K值和距离度量，观察KNN图如何变化。注意同类型细胞之间的边（内部连接）和不同类型之间的边（外部连接）。
ch5.step2WhatDesc: K近邻图将每个细胞连接到其K个最相似的邻居。这个图捕获了数据的局部结构——同类型细胞应该彼此相连。
ch5.step3ModularityDesc: 模块度Q衡量社区划分的质量。Q=0表示随机划分，Q>0.3通常表示有意义的社区结构。Louvain通过贪婪优化最大化Q。
ch5.step3TryDesc: 观察Louvain算法迭代优化社区分配。每轮尝试将节点移动到邻居社区，如果能提高模块度Q就接受。
ch5.step3WhatDesc: Louvain是一种贪婪的社区发现算法。它通过迭代地将节点分配到使模块度Q增益最大的社区来工作。
ch5.step4ConfusionDesc: 行是预测的聚类，列是真实的细胞类型。对角线上的值高表示聚类与真实类型匹配良好。
ch5.step4Try1: 调整分辨率参数，观察聚类数量变化
ch5.step4Try2: 比较ARI和NMI在不同K值下的表现
ch5.step4Try3: 检查混淆矩阵，找出哪些细胞类型被混淆了

ch6.title: t-SNE & UMAP
ch6.subtitleFull: 从线性到非线性。学习t-SNE和UMAP如何将弯曲数据展开成可读的2D地图。
ch6.step1Name: 线性 vs 非线性降维
ch6.step1WhyDesc: PCA找到最佳的直线投影。但如果数据有弯曲结构，线性方法会把层次压在一起。
ch6.step1TryDesc: 下方三图展示同一数据集的三种降维结果。
ch6.step2CoreDesc: t-SNE将距离转化为概率，然后最小化高维和低维概率的差异。
ch6.step2TryDesc: 按播放看点从随机位置移动到簇。调整Perplexity观察影响。
ch6.step3CoreDesc: UMAP在高维构建拓扑图，然后在2D中重建类似拓扑。
ch6.step4CompareDesc: 多次运行t-SNE——注意每次结果不同。UMAP和PCA更稳定。
ch6.step4Pitfall3: 不要在t-SNE坐标上直接聚类
ch6.step4Pitfall4: 点的密度不代表真实细胞密度
ch6.subtitle: 从高维空间到2D地图。通过瑞士卷动画理解t-SNE和UMAP。

zh.footerBuilt: 使用 Next.js + p5.js 构建
